section.title: The Seven Days
section.sectionRationale: The creation prologue is the opening movement before the first toledot heading at Genesis 2:4, so 1:1 to 2:3 is one deliberate unit sealed by the seventh day rest.
section.thesis: Before the drama of a fallen world begins, God speaks a formless void into an ordered, good, and habitable home; this is act one of the whole story.
section.frameNote: Two panels. Days one to three form the realms, light, sky and sea, land. Days four to six fill them. The seventh day stands apart as the goal.

verses.text: In the {{beginning|beginning}} {{elohim|God}} {{bara|created}} the heavens and the earth.
Now the earth was formless and void, and darkness was over the surface of the deep. And the Spirit of God was {{hover|hovering}} over the surface of the waters.
And God said, “Let there be {{light|light}},” and there was light.
And by the seventh day God had finished the work He had been doing; so on that day He {{rest|rested}} from all His work.
Then God blessed the seventh day and sanctified it, because on that day He rested from all the work of creation that He had accomplished.

beginning.gloss: The first, the head of a thing. The whole book unfolds what this one word opens: there was a start, and God stood before it.

elohim.gloss: A plural form joined to a singular verb. It signals majesty and fullness, and many hear a first quiet hint of the plurality later revealed as Father, Son, and Spirit.

bara.gloss: To create. In the Hebrew Bible this verb takes only God as its subject; whatever creating is happening here, it is a work no creature can do.

hover.gloss: To hover or flutter, the same verb used of an eagle over its young in Deuteronomy 32:11. The Spirit is attentively near the formless deep, poised to bring life.

light.gloss: Light, made on day one, before the sun on day four. The point is theological before astronomical: light comes from God. Paul later reads this as a picture of the new birth.

rest.gloss: To cease, to rest, not from exhaustion but the settled rest of a finished good work. It gives us Sabbath and points forward to the rest that remains for God's people.

context.literary: The prologue to Genesis and to the Torah, opening the first of the book's toledot units. The genre is exalted Hebrew narrative with poetic cadence, not myth and not a science manual.
context.historical: Traditionally Moses, writing to Israel newly out of Egypt among Egyptian and Mesopotamian creation myths. Read against that backdrop it is polemic: sun, moon, sea, and man are not gods but creatures of the one God.
context.canonical: The Bible's first page, establishing creation as the act on which fall, redemption, and new creation all depend.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal God as the sole sovereign Creator who orders chaos into a cosmos fit to be His dwelling. The text signals its own structure of forming, filling, and rest.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes God's acts. The prescriptive threads are the Sabbath pattern, later grounded in Exodus 20:11, and the mandate to image bearers.

debates.question: What are the days?

views.label: Twenty four hour
views.case: The plain sequence of evening and morning.

views.label: Framework
views.case: The forming and filling pattern is literary, not a timetable.

views.label: Day age or analogical
views.case: Yom carries a longer or analogical span.

debates.bearing: None of these touches the main claim, that God authored everything that is.

study.originalLanguages: The load bearing words move as a set: bara marks a divine only act, Elohim holds majesty, the Spirit rachaph hovers with life, and God names what He makes, which in the Ancient Near East is an act of dominion.

typology.fulfillment: A type of the greater rest that remains for God's people in Hebrews 4.

typology.type: Eden as the first dwelling of God with man
typology.fulfillment: Prefigures tabernacle, temple, and the new creation.

typology.type: Adam the image bearer given dominion
typology.fulfillment: A pattern of the true Man to come, Romans 5:14.

study.christ: John opens by echoing this text, in the beginning was the Word, all things made through Him, John 1:1-3. Paul calls the Son the image of the invisible God in whom all things were created, Colossians 1:15-16, and applies day one to conversion, the God who said let light shine out of darkness has shone in our hearts, 2 Corinthians 4:6.
study.god: Sovereign and powerful, creating by word alone. Good, and calling His work good. Orderly, personal, and generous. Transcendent over creation yet immanent within it. A God who works and then rests, dignifying both.

crossReferences.note: Deliberately reruns Genesis 1:1 with the Word as agent of creation.
Paul quotes day one to describe the new birth.
The Sabbath command is grounded in the seventh day.
The motifs of light, image, and rest reach their resolution in the new creation.

study.oneStory: This is creation, the first act. The good world fractures in chapter three, but the same Creator calls Israel, comes as Christ, and makes a new heavens and new earth where the seventh day rest becomes unending. The very good about to be lost is what the whole Bible strains to see restored.

sermon.why: No link verified in this session. Candidates worth verifying for this passage include Edmund Clowney, D.A. Carson, or Sinclair Ferguson on Genesis 1.

translationNotes.note: Some translations render an initial dependent clause, when God began to create, rather than in the beginning God created. The difference bears on whether verse 1 is the first act or a summary title.

questions: God is the source of all things, the fountain of living waters. Where are you tempted to drink from cracked cisterns, the substitutes that never satisfy, instead of from Him?
The Spirit hovered over the formless deep, and God brought order by His word. What in your life feels formless right now, and what would it look like to submit it to His word this week?
God worked, then rested, and called it good. Does your rhythm of work and rest trust His finished work, or your own striving? Name one change that would honour His pattern.
You are made in God's image and given dominion. How does that dignity change the way you approach your ordinary work, whether behind a wheel, at a desk, or in a home?
On the first day God said, let there be light, before the sun existed, because light comes from Him. Where do you need to ask God to speak light into a dark place?
